items.title: Spam filter trained on labeled emails
items.scenario: A team collects 50,000 emails labeled spam or not-spam. A logistic model learns word weights from the training set so P(spam | email text) is high on phrases like "claim prize" and low on internal company threads. After training, new inbox messages are scored without anyone adding new keyword rules.
items.explanation: The filter **learns from data**: parameters are induced from labeled examples rather than a fixed blocklist maintained by hand.

items.title: House price prediction from past sales
items.scenario: A real-estate app has historical records: square footage, bedrooms, zip code, and sold price. Linear regression fits weights so predicted price tracks actual sales. When a user lists a new home, the model outputs an estimate from learned coefficients—no appraiser rule table for every neighborhood.
items.explanation: Supervised ML maps **features** to a **numeric target** learned from past transactions.

items.title: Product recommendations from click history
items.scenario: A streaming service logs which shows each user watched and rated. A collaborative filtering model learns latent taste vectors from millions of interactions. It suggests titles similar users enjoyed—patterns no product manager could encode as static rules.
items.explanation: Behavioral logs are **experience E**; recommendation quality **P** improves as more interaction data arrives.

items.title: When rules beat learning (tiny, stable domain)
items.scenario: A payroll system must compute overtime as 1.5x hourly rate after 40 hours. The formula is exact, auditable, and unchanged for years. A team skips ML because there is no ambiguous pattern to discover—explicit logic is cheaper and correct by construction.
items.explanation: ML shines when the mapping from inputs to outputs is **too complex or shifting** to maintain as rules; deterministic formulas are still the right tool when the law is known.
